question: What are the major programming paradigms?

options: Imperative/procedural (commands changing state), object-oriented (objects bundling state and behaviour), functional (pure functions, immutability, no side effects), declarative/logic (specify what, not how) — most modern languages are multi-paradigm, blending these styles
There is only one way to program (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
All languages are purely object-oriented (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence)
Paradigms are interchangeable with no conceptual difference (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Programming paradigms are fundamental styles of structuring and thinking about computation, each with characteristic concepts and trade-offs. Imperative programming describes computation as a sequence of statements that change program state (variables, memory) — 'how to do it' step by step; procedural programming organises imperative code into reusable procedures/functions (C, Pascal). Object-oriented programming (OOP) bundles state (data/fields) and behaviour (methods) into objects, organised via classes, with pillars of encapsulation (hiding internal state behind interfaces), inheritance (deriving new types from existing ones), and polymorphism (uniform interfaces over different types) — modelling problems as interacting objects (Java, C++, Smalltalk). Functional programming treats computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, emphasising pure functions (output depends only on input, no side effects), immutability (data is not modified in place), first-class and higher-order functions, and avoiding shared mutable state — yielding code that is easier to reason about, test, parallelise, and compose (Haskell, Lisp, and functional features in modern languages); concepts include map/filter/reduce, recursion, closures, and (in pure FP) controlling effects via constructs like monads. Declarative programming specifies what result is wanted rather than how to compute it, leaving the 'how' to the system: logic programming (Prolog — state facts and rules, query for results via inference), and SQL (declare the desired data, the optimiser figures out execution) are examples. Most modern languages are multi-paradigm, deliberately blending styles — Python and JavaScript support procedural, OO, and functional styles; C++ spans procedural, OO, generic, and functional; Scala and Rust mix OO and functional. The value of understanding paradigms is conceptual flexibility: different problems suit different styles (OO for modelling stateful entities and large systems; functional for data transformation, concurrency, and correctness; declarative for queries and rule-based logic), and good programmers choose and combine paradigms appropriately. Paradigms shape not just syntax but how one decomposes problems and reasons about programs, making them a core topic in understanding programming languages.

active.prompt: Match each PL concept with its meaning:

pairs.definition: Types checked at compile time (catch errors early, e.g. Java/C++) vs at runtime (flexible, e.g. Python)
Translated to machine code ahead of time vs executed by an interpreter/VM at runtime
Output depends only on inputs, no side effects; referentially transparent, easy to reason about
A function capturing variables from its enclosing scope; carries its environment

active.explanation: Programming-language theory studies type systems (static/dynamic, strong/weak, inference), execution models (compiled/interpreted/JIT), evaluation strategies, and abstraction mechanisms. These design choices shape a language's safety, performance, expressiveness, and the kinds of errors caught early versus late.

question: What is the difference between static and dynamic typing?

options: Static typing checks types at compile time (errors caught before running, better tooling/performance, more upfront annotation — Java, C++, Rust); dynamic typing checks at runtime (more flexible and concise, faster prototyping, but type errors surface only when executed — Python, JavaScript)
Static typing means types never change; dynamic means they change randomly (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Dynamic typing catches all errors at compile time (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
They produce identical guarantees and trade-offs (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Static versus dynamic typing is one of the most consequential design dimensions of programming languages, governing when type correctness is checked. In statically-typed languages (Java, C++, C#, Rust, Go, Haskell), the type of every expression is known and checked at compile time, before the program runs. This catches a large class of errors early (type mismatches, calling missing methods, passing wrong argument types) without executing the code, enables powerful tooling (precise autocomplete, refactoring, jump-to-definition), allows the compiler to optimise using type information (often faster execution), and serves as machine-checked documentation. The cost is more upfront effort (type annotations) and sometimes verbosity or rigidity — though modern type inference (Haskell, Rust, Scala, and increasingly Java/C++ with auto/var) lets the compiler deduce many types, reducing annotation burden. In dynamically-typed languages (Python, JavaScript, Ruby, Lisp), types are associated with values at runtime and checked as the program executes; variables can hold any type and a type error (e.g. calling a string method on a number) is detected only when that line actually runs. This offers flexibility and concision (no type declarations, easy duck typing and metaprogramming, rapid prototyping) at the cost of errors surfacing late (possibly in production), weaker tooling, and often slower execution (runtime type checks/dispatch). The distinction is separate from strong vs weak typing (how strictly types are enforced / how much implicit coercion occurs) — Python is dynamically but strongly typed, C is statically but relatively weakly typed. The industry trend has been to blend the approaches for the best of both: gradual/optional typing adds static checking to dynamic languages (Python type hints checked by mypy/pyright, TypeScript adding static types to JavaScript), letting teams get early error detection and tooling while retaining flexibility. The trade-off — early safety and performance and tooling (static) versus flexibility and rapid development (dynamic) — is a recurring theme, and the choice depends on project scale, performance needs, team size, and how critical early error detection is (large, long-lived, safety-critical codebases tend to favour static typing). Understanding it is central to programming-language theory and to choosing the right tool for a task.

question: How do compiled and interpreted languages differ?

options: A compiler translates source to machine code ahead of time (fast execution, errors caught at compile time, platform-specific binary — C, C++, Rust); an interpreter executes source directly at runtime (portable, flexible, faster edit-run, but slower — Python, Ruby); many use hybrids (bytecode + VM, JIT) like Java and JavaScript
Compiled languages run more slowly than interpreted ones (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Interpreters translate everything to machine code before running (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
The distinction has no effect on performance or portability (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: The compiled-versus-interpreted distinction concerns how source code is translated and executed, with significant consequences for performance, portability, and the development experience — though it is more accurately a spectrum than a strict dichotomy. A compiler translates the entire source program ahead of time into machine code (native binary) for a specific processor/platform; the resulting executable runs directly on the hardware. This yields fast execution (the heavy translation and optimisation work is done once, before running), catches many errors at compile time (especially with static typing), but produces platform-specific binaries (must recompile for different architectures/OSes) and has a slower edit-compile-run cycle. Classic compiled languages: C, C++, Rust, Go. An interpreter, by contrast, reads and executes the source program directly (statement by statement) at runtime, without producing a separate machine-code executable. This gives portability (the same source runs anywhere the interpreter exists), flexibility (dynamic features, immediate execution, easy REPL/scripting, fast edit-run cycle), and convenience, at the cost of slower execution (translation overhead repeated during running) and errors often surfacing only at runtime. Classic interpreted languages: Python, Ruby, classic JavaScript, shell scripts. In practice, most modern languages are hybrids that blur the line: many compile source to an intermediate bytecode that runs on a virtual machine (Java compiles to JVM bytecode; C# to .NET CIL; Python to .pyc bytecode run by the CPython VM) — combining portability (the bytecode is platform-independent, the VM is ported) with reasonable performance. Just-in-time (JIT) compilation goes further, compiling hot (frequently-executed) bytecode to native machine code at runtime, profiling and optimising based on actual execution — used by the JVM (HotSpot), JavaScript engines (V8), and PyPy, often approaching or matching ahead-of-time-compiled performance while retaining portability and dynamic flexibility. Ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation, transpilation (source-to-source, e.g. TypeScript to JavaScript), and mixed strategies further populate the spectrum. The practical implications: compiled/AOT for maximum performance and deployment as standalone binaries (systems, performance-critical software); interpreted for scripting, rapid development, and portability; bytecode+VM and JIT for a balance of portability, productivity, and performance (enterprise applications, web). Understanding where a language sits on this spectrum explains its performance characteristics, deployment model, startup time, and development workflow, and is a core topic in how programming languages are implemented.

question: What is functional programming and why is it valued?

options: A paradigm treating computation as evaluation of pure functions, emphasising immutability, no side effects, first-class/higher-order functions, and referential transparency; valued because such code is easier to reason about, test, compose, and parallelise (no shared mutable state) — influencing mainstream languages
Programming using only built-in functions (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
A style that relies heavily on mutable global state (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
An obsolete paradigm with no modern relevance (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: Functional programming (FP) is a paradigm that models computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions and avoids changing state and mutable data, in contrast to imperative programming's focus on sequences of state-changing commands. Its core principles: pure functions — a function's output depends only on its inputs, with no side effects (it doesn't modify external state, perform I/O, or depend on anything hidden), making it referentially transparent (a call can be replaced by its result without changing behaviour); immutability — data structures are not modified in place but transformed into new values, eliminating a whole class of bugs from shared mutable state; first-class and higher-order functions — functions are values that can be passed as arguments, returned, and stored, enabling powerful abstractions (map, filter, reduce/fold, function composition); and often declarative expression, recursion (instead of mutable loops), closures (functions capturing their environment), and lazy evaluation. Pure FP languages (Haskell) enforce purity and manage side effects explicitly through constructs like monads (which sequence effectful computations while keeping the core pure); other languages (Lisp/Scheme/Clojure, ML/OCaml/F#, Erlang/Elixir, Scala) embrace FP to varying degrees. FP is valued for several reasons. Reasoning and correctness: pure functions are easy to understand, test (same input always gives same output, no setup/teardown of state), and verify, because there is no hidden state or action at a distance; referential transparency enables equational reasoning and aggressive compiler optimisation. Concurrency and parallelism: with no shared mutable state, there are no data races — pure functional code parallelises safely and naturally, a major advantage in the multi-core era (and why FP ideas underpin big-data frameworks like MapReduce/Spark and reliable concurrent systems like Erlang's actor model). Composability and modularity: higher-order functions and composition build complex behaviour from small, reusable pieces. FP's influence on the mainstream has been profound: lambdas/closures, immutable collections, streams, map/filter/reduce, and Optionals/pattern matching have been adopted by Java, C++, C#, Python, JavaScript, Swift, Kotlin, and Rust, so even programmers in 'non-functional' languages now use functional techniques daily. The trade-offs — immutability can have performance/memory costs (mitigated by persistent data structures and optimisation), and pure I/O-free models require machinery (monads) to interact with the messy stateful world — are generally outweighed in many domains by the gains in clarity, testability, and safe concurrency. FP represents a different and increasingly central way of thinking about programs: building reliable software from composable, side-effect-free transformations of immutable data.

question: What does a compiler do, and what are its main phases?

options: It translates source code to a target (machine code/bytecode) through phases: lexical analysis (tokens), syntax analysis (parse tree via a grammar), semantic analysis (type checking), intermediate representation, optimisation, and code generation — turning human-readable programs into efficient executable form
It only checks spelling in source files (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general)
It executes the program line by line without translation (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence)
Compilation is a single indivisible step with no structure (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: A compiler is a program that translates source code written in a high-level language into a lower-level target representation (machine code, assembly, or bytecode), typically performing analysis and optimisation along the way. Its work is classically organised into a pipeline of phases, often grouped into a 'front end' (analysis, language-specific) and a 'back end' (synthesis, target-specific), connected by an intermediate representation. The main phases: (1) Lexical analysis (scanning/lexing) — the lexer reads the raw character stream and groups it into tokens (keywords, identifiers, literals, operators, punctuation), discarding whitespace/comments; this is done with finite automata / regular languages. (2) Syntax analysis (parsing) — the parser checks that the token sequence conforms to the language's grammar (a context-free grammar) and builds a parse tree / abstract syntax tree (AST) representing the program's hierarchical structure; this uses pushdown-automaton-based algorithms (LL, LR). (3) Semantic analysis — checks meaning that grammar can't capture: type checking, scope resolution (matching uses of names to declarations), ensuring variables are declared before use, checking function signatures, building a symbol table; this is where type errors are reported. (4) Intermediate code generation — the AST is translated into an intermediate representation (IR), a lower-level, machine-independent form (like three-address code or SSA) convenient for analysis and optimisation. (5) Optimisation — the IR is transformed to improve performance or reduce size while preserving behaviour: constant folding, dead-code elimination, common-subexpression elimination, loop optimisations, inlining, register allocation strategies, etc. (machine-independent and later machine-dependent optimisation). (6) Code generation — the optimised IR is translated into the target language (machine code for a specific architecture, or bytecode), including instruction selection, register allocation, and instruction scheduling. The result is then assembled/linked into an executable. This phased architecture is powerful and modular: the front end depends on the source language and the back end on the target machine, connected through the IR — so supporting a new language means writing a new front end, and targeting a new processor means a new back end, with optimisation shared (the basis of frameworks like LLVM, where many languages compile to a common IR optimised and emitted to many targets). Compiler theory beautifully applies the theory of computation (regular languages for lexing, context-free grammars and automata for parsing), draws on graph algorithms and optimisation for the middle/back end, and is foundational to all software — every program in a compiled language passes through this pipeline. Interpreters and JIT compilers share the front-end analysis phases but differ in execution. Understanding compiler phases illuminates how high-level code becomes efficient machine instructions, why certain errors are caught at certain stages, and how language features are implemented.

options: A deep isomorphism between logic and computation: propositions correspond to types, and proofs correspond to programs — proving a theorem is constructing a program of the corresponding type; it underlies type theory, proof assistants (Coq, Lean, Agda), and dependently-typed languages
A method for converting code into flowcharts (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence)
An algorithm for type inference only (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
A rule for naming functions (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: The Curry-Howard correspondence (also called the proofs-as-programs or propositions-as-types principle) is a profound and beautiful isomorphism revealing that mathematical logic and computation are two views of the same underlying structure. Discovered by Haskell Curry and William Howard, it establishes a precise correspondence: propositions (logical statements) correspond to types, and proofs of a proposition correspond to programs (terms) of the corresponding type. Concretely, in the simplest form (intuitionistic propositional logic and simply-typed lambda calculus): logical implication A ⇒ B corresponds to the function type A → B (a proof of the implication is a function transforming a proof of A into a proof of B); conjunction A ∧ B corresponds to a product/pair type A × B (a proof is a pair of proofs); disjunction A ∨ B corresponds to a sum/union type (a proof is a tagged choice of one side); true corresponds to the unit type, false to the empty type; and the act of proving a theorem corresponds exactly to constructing a (well-typed) program inhabiting that type. Moreover, proof normalisation (simplifying a proof) corresponds to program evaluation (reducing/running the term) — logic's cut-elimination is computation. This is not a mere analogy but a rigorous structural isomorphism, and it extends: predicate (first-order) logic corresponds to dependent types, where types can depend on values (enabling types like 'a list of length n' or 'a sorted list'); higher-order logic, modal logics, and linear logic have computational counterparts too. The consequences are deep and practical. It underpins type theory as a foundation for mathematics (an alternative to set theory — e.g. Martin-Löf type theory and homotopy type theory). It is the theoretical basis of proof assistants / interactive theorem provers — Coq, Lean, Agda, Idris — where one writes mathematical proofs as programs whose types are the theorems, and the type checker verifies the proof's correctness mechanically; this has enabled fully machine-checked proofs of major results (the four-colour theorem, the Feit-Thompson theorem, the Kepler conjecture) and the formal verification of software and hardware (proving programs correct by construction). It informs the design of dependently-typed and richly-typed programming languages, where the type system can express and enforce strong correctness properties ('well-typed programs can't go wrong' taken to an extreme — types as specifications). And it embodies a unifying philosophical insight: logic, computation, and (via further extensions to category theory and topology) even geometry are deeply intertwined — 'a proof is a program, and the formula it proves is the type for the program.' The Curry-Howard correspondence is one of the most elegant bridges in all of science, connecting the foundations of mathematics, logic, and computer science, and it actively shapes the cutting edge of formal verification and language design.
